aiSummary: 《麻省理工科技评论》洞察报告指出，企业AI应用正从试点转向生产，但全面成功仍面临挑战。报告基于2025年底对500名美国中大型企业IT高管的调查，发现缺乏集成平台、数据孤岛和治理问题是主要障碍。拥有企业级集成平台的公司，其AI工作流使用多样化数据源的可能性高出五倍，且更易实现跨部门部署和自动化。报告警告，若无稳固的运营基础，到2027年超40%的智能体AI项目可能因成本、准确性和治理问题被取消。
aiInterpretation: 根据《麻省理工科技评论》洞察部门发布的最新报告，企业人工智能的应用正在加速，但实现全面运营成功仍存在显著差距。许多组织正从试点项目转向生产环境，并开始尝试更具自主性的“智能体AI”。然而，调查显示，缺乏集成的数据和系统、稳定的自动化工作流程以及治理模型，正阻碍AI计划规模化。报告基于2025年12月对500名美国中大型企业资深IT领导者的调研，揭示了构建坚实运营基础的重要性。 为什么重要：企业AI的成功不再仅仅取决于算法模型本身，而更依赖于将AI与现有业务流程和数据系统深度整合的“运营基础”。没有这个基础，AI项目容易陷入试点困境，无法产生真正的商业价值。报告预测，到2027年，超过40%的智能体AI项目可能因成本、准确性和治理挑战而被取消，凸显了解决这一“运营鸿沟”的紧迫性。 影响面： 1. 技术：企业级集成平台成为关键技术，能帮助组织避免重复建设和数据孤岛，为日益自主的AI工作流提供清晰的可视化与管理。 2. 商业：拥有集成平台的公司更可能成功部署AI，从而获得竞争优势。反之，AI项目失败将导致直接的投资损失和机会成本。 3. 产业链：对可靠集成平台和解决方案的需求将推动企业软件和服务市场的发展，可能催生新的合作伙伴关系与商业模式。 4. 监管/安全：随着AI自主性增强，治理模型和合规性变得至关重要。缺乏治理可能导致项目因监管或安全风险而中止。 5. 用户/社会：在企业内部，成功的AI集成能提升员工效率并改变工作方式；而在社会层面，AI应用的稳健推进影响着公众对这项技术的信任与接受度。
aiExplanation: 简单来说，很多公司都在用AI，但光有AI技术不够，必须把它和公司里已有的各种软件、数据和业务流程很好地“连接”和“管理”起来，否则AI项目很容易失败，钱就白花了。
aiBroadcastScript:
[这条是什么]
《麻省理工科技评论》发布报告，指出企业应用AI时，面临从试点项目扩展到全公司范围的“运营鸿沟”。

[翻成人话]
很多公司试水AI成功了，但想在全公司铺开用起来却很难，因为缺一个能把AI和公司里现有各种系统、数据打通并管好的“底座”。

[为什么重要]
如果解决不了这个“连接”和“管理”的问题，超过40%的AI项目可能会因为太烧钱、效果不准或管理混乱而在未来几年被砍掉，导致巨大浪费。

[下一步确认点]
企业需要评估自身是否拥有或需要建设一个企业级的集成平台，并组建专门的团队来负责AI工作流的长期维护和治理。

title: The Download: Earth’s rumblings, and AI for strikes on Iran
summary: This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology. Listen to Earth’s rumbling, secret soundtrack The boom of a calving glacier. The crackling rumble of a wildfire. The roar of a surging storm front. They’re the noises of the living…
content: The Download The Download: Earth’s rumblings, and AI for strikes on Iran Plus: How Anthropic’s AI is being used for US strikes on Iran. By Charlotte Jee archive page March 4, 2026 This is today's edition of The Download , our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what's going on in the world of technology. Listen to Earth’s rumbling, secret soundtrack The boom of a calving glacier. The crackling rumble of a wildfire. The roar of a surging storm front. They’re the noises of the living Earth, but as loud as all these things are, they emit even more acoustic energy below the threshold of human hearing, at frequencies of 20 hertz or lower. These “infrasounds” have such long wavelengths that they can travel around the globe as churning emanations of distant events. But humans have never been able to hear them. Until now. Read our story and check the sounds out for yourself . —Monique Brouillette This story is from the latest March/April issue of our print magazine, all about crime. Subscribe today to get full access. You’ll also receive an in-depth digital AI report and an exclusive e-book on how to understand AI’s reckoning. MIT Technology Review Narrated: The curious case of the disappearing Lamborghinis A new wave of theft is rocking the luxury car industry—mixing high tech with old-school chop-shop techniques to snag vehicles while they’re in transport. It’s remained under the radar, even as it’s rocked the industry over the past two years. MIT Technology Review identified more than a dozen cases involving high-end vehicles, obtained court records, and spoke to law enforcement, brokers, drivers, and victims in multiple states to reveal how transport fraud is wreaking havoc across the country. This is our latest story to be turned into a MIT Technology Review Narrated podcast, which we’re publishing each week on Spotify and Apple Podcasts . Just navigate to MIT Technology Review Narrated on either platform, and follow us to get all our new content as it’s released. The must-reads I’ve combed the internet to find you today’s most fun/important/scary/fascinating stories about technology. 1 How Anthropic’s AI tool Claude is being used for US strikes on Iran It’s helping to identify targets and prioritize them—for now. ( WP $) + We should all be alarmed by the White House turning on Anthropic. ( The Atlantic $) + OpenAI is pursuing a contract with NATO. ( Reuters ) 2 Iran’s Shahed drones give it a major advantage They’re cheap and easy to manufacture, but very expensive to intercept. ( CNBC ) + The US is manufacturing copies of the drone to use against Iran. ( New Scientist $) + Israel’s plot to kill Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was years in the making. ( FT $) 3 Data center politics are getting an early test in North Carolina One of the candidates is calling for a 10-year national moratorium on building them. ( The Guardian ) + But it’s not just data centers that are driving people’s electricity bills up. ( Inside Climate News ) + Data centers are amazing. Everyone hates them. ( MIT Technology Review ) + Never mind space—why not just build them into floating offshore wind turbines? ( IEEE Spectrum ) 4 LLMs can unmask pseudonymous users At a speed and scale far beyond what even skilled human investigators can manage. ( Ars Technica ) + It’s also very easy to persuade them to fabricate scientific papers. ( Nature $) 5 TikTok has ruled out end-to-end encryption, citing user safety It’s a stance that sets it apart from almost all rival social media services. ( BBC ) + The strategy will please parents, police—and hackers. ( Cybernews ) + TikTok is experiencing Oracle-related server issues, again. ( Gizmodo ) 6 Why is SpaceX going public? One thing seems certain: it’s not for the reasons Musk’s claiming. ( The Verge $) + Two companies have just unveiled plans to build lunar harvesters . ( Ars Technica ) 7 NASA’s scheduled its next attempt to launch the Artemis II moon rocket On April Fool’s Day, of all days. Good luck! ( Space ) 8 What it’s like to live with a brain implant for years 🧠 For 65-year-old Rodney Gorham, who can no longer walk, talk, or move his hands, it’s been a real lifeline. ( Wired $) + This patient’s Neuralink brain implant is getting a boost from generative AI. ( MIT Technology Review ) 9 Pokémon Pokopia is getting rave reviews It apparently mixes Animal Crossing and Stardew Valley, with a hint of Minecraft-style building. ( BBC ) 10. Hollywood is scouring YouTube for its next horror hits 🔪 Movie studios want to bring the threat from the platform in-house. ( The New Yorker $) + One YouTuber’s self-financed horror flick opened at 4,000 theatres. ( Variety ) Quote of the day “I think it just looked opportunistic and sloppy.” —OpenAI CEO Sam Altman comments on X about his decision to rush in to work with the US Department of War after its talks with Anthropic fell apart. One More Thing Crypto millionaires are pouring money into Central America to build their own cities El Salvador’s Conchagua Volcano, home to a lush ecotourism retreat amid its sun-dappled forest, is set to host a glittering new Bitcoin City, according to the country’s president. While some politicians and residents believe in crypto’s potential to jump-start the economy, others see history repeating itself. They also question who these projects are really for, and whether the countries serving as test beds will truly benefit. Read the full story . —Laurie Clarke We can still have nice things A place for comfort, fun and distraction to brighten up your day. (Got any ideas? Drop me a line or skeet 'em at me .) + Art is everywhere in Los Angeles : you just need to know what you’re looking for. + Survivor has been running for 50 seasons. How is that even possible ?! + MP3 players are cool again. I don’t make the rules. + Be careful out there—you never know when you’re going to come across a Homer Simpson AI cover song . fullText: The Download The Download: Earth’s rumblings, and AI for strikes on Iran Plus: How Anthropic’s AI is being used for US strikes on Iran. By Charlotte Jee archive page March 4, 2026 This is today's edition of The Download , our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what's going on in the world of technology. Listen to Earth’s rumbling, secret soundtrack The boom of a calving glacier. The crackling rumble of a wildfire. The roar of a surging storm front. They’re the noises of the living Earth, but as loud as all these things are, they emit even more acoustic energy below the threshold of human hearing, at frequencies of 20 hertz or lower. These “infrasounds” have such long wavelengths that they can travel around the globe as churning emanations of distant events. But humans have never been able to hear them. Until now. Read our story and check the sounds out for yourself . —Monique Brouillette This story is from the latest March/April issue of our print magazine, all about crime. Subscribe today to get full access. You’ll also receive an in-depth digital AI report and an exclusive e-book on how to understand AI’s reckoning. MIT Technology Review Narrated: The curious case of the disappearing Lamborghinis A new wave of theft is rocking the luxury car industry—mixing high tech with old-school chop-shop techniques to snag vehicles while they’re in transport. It’s remained under the radar, even as it’s rocked the industry over the past two years. MIT Technology Review identified more than a dozen cases involving high-end vehicles, obtained court records, and spoke to law enforcement, brokers, drivers, and victims in multiple states to reveal how transport fraud is wreaking havoc across the country. This is our latest story to be turned into a MIT Technology Review Narrated podcast, which we’re publishing each week on Spotify and Apple Podcasts . Just navigate to MIT Technology Review Narrated on either platform, and follow us to get all our new content as it’s released. The must-reads I’ve combed the internet to find you today’s most fun/important/scary/fascinating stories about technology. 1 How Anthropic’s AI tool Claude is being used for US strikes on Iran It’s helping to identify targets and prioritize them—for now. ( WP $) + We should all be alarmed by the White House turning on Anthropic. ( The Atlantic $) + OpenAI is pursuing a contract with NATO. ( Reuters ) 2 Iran’s Shahed drones give it a major advantage They’re cheap and easy to manufacture, but very expensive to intercept. ( CNBC ) + The US is manufacturing copies of the drone to use against Iran. ( New Scientist $) + Israel’s plot to kill Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was years in the making. ( FT $) 3 Data center politics are getting an early test in North Carolina One of the candidates is calling for a 10-year national moratorium on building them. ( The Guardian ) + But it’s not just data centers that are driving people’s electricity bills up. ( Inside Climate News ) + Data centers are amazing. Everyone hates them. ( MIT Technology Review ) + Never mind space—why not just build them into floating offshore wind turbines? ( IEEE Spectrum ) 4 LLMs can unmask pseudonymous users At a speed and scale far beyond what even skilled human investigators can manage. ( Ars Technica ) + It’s also very easy to persuade them to fabricate scientific papers. ( Nature $) 5 TikTok has ruled out end-to-end encryption, citing user safety It’s a stance that sets it apart from almost all rival social media services. ( BBC ) + The strategy will please parents, police—and hackers. ( Cybernews ) + TikTok is experiencing Oracle-related server issues, again. ( Gizmodo ) 6 Why is SpaceX going public? One thing seems certain: it’s not for the reasons Musk’s claiming. ( The Verge $) + Two companies have just unveiled plans to build lunar harvesters . ( Ars Technica ) 7 NASA’s scheduled its next attempt to launch the Artemis II moon rocket On April Fool’s Day, of all days. Good luck! ( Space ) 8 What it’s like to live with a brain implant for years 🧠 For 65-year-old Rodney Gorham, who can no longer walk, talk, or move his hands, it’s been a real lifeline. ( Wired $) + This patient’s Neuralink brain implant is getting a boost from generative AI. ( MIT Technology Review ) 9 Pokémon Pokopia is getting rave reviews It apparently mixes Animal Crossing and Stardew Valley, with a hint of Minecraft-style building. ( BBC ) 10. Hollywood is scouring YouTube for its next horror hits 🔪 Movie studios want to bring the threat from the platform in-house. ( The New Yorker $) + One YouTuber’s self-financed horror flick opened at 4,000 theatres. ( Variety ) Quote of the day “I think it just looked opportunistic and sloppy.” —OpenAI CEO Sam Altman comments on X about his decision to rush in to work with the US Department of War after its talks with Anthropic fell apart. One More Thing Crypto millionaires are pouring money into Central America to build their own cities El Salvador’s Conchagua Volcano, home to a lush ecotourism retreat amid its sun-dappled forest, is set to host a glittering new Bitcoin City, according to the country’s president. While some politicians and residents believe in crypto’s potential to jump-start the economy, others see history repeating itself. They also question who these projects are really for, and whether the countries serving as test beds will truly benefit. Read the full story . —Laurie Clarke We can still have nice things A place for comfort, fun and distraction to brighten up your day. (Got any ideas? Drop me a line or skeet 'em at me .) + Art is everywhere in Los Angeles : you just need to know what you’re looking for. + Survivor has been running for 50 seasons. How is that even possible ?! + MP3 players are cool again. I don’t make the rules. + Be careful out there—you never know when you’re going to come across a Homer Simpson AI cover song . aiSummary: 美国军方正使用Anthropic公司的人工智能工具Claude来协助识别和优先打击伊朗目标，这标志着AI在军事决策中的角色日益关键。与此同时，地球的次声波首次被转化为人类可听的声音，揭示了星球不为人知的‘声音景观’。这两项进展分别指向了AI在国家安全领域的应用深化，以及对地球环境监测方式的革新。
aiInterpretation: 近期科技领域有两项引人注目的进展。首先，美国军方被曝正在使用人工智能公司Anthropic开发的AI工具Claude，协助分析情报并识别、优先排序针对伊朗的打击目标。这标志着生成式AI正从商业和科研领域，快速渗透至国家安全和军事决策的核心环节。 为什么重要？ 将AI用于军事目标识别，极大地提升了情报处理和决策的速度与规模，可能改变现代冲突的形态。然而，这也引发了关于算法偏见、问责机制以及AI自主性边界的深刻伦理与安全担忧。 影响面 从技术角度看，这要求AI模型具备极高的准确性、鲁棒性和可解释性，以应对复杂多变的战场环境。商业上，它为AI公司开辟了庞大的政府与国防市场，但也可能引发关于技术中立性的争议。产业链方面，涉及从基础模型研发、军事系统集成到情报分析的全链条协作。在监管/安全维度，这迫切要求国际社会建立关于致命性自主武器系统（LAWS）和军事AI使用的规范与条约。对用户/社会而言，公众对AI技术被用于战争可能感到不安，并担忧‘算法战争’降低开战门槛，加剧全球不稳定。 另一项进展是，科学家首次成功将地球产生的、人耳无法听见的次声波（如冰川崩解、野火、风暴的声音）转化为可听见的音频，让我们得以‘聆听’地球的隐秘‘心跳’。这项技术有望用于更精准、实时地监测环境变化与自然灾害。
aiExplanation: 简单说，就是美国用AI来帮忙决定打哪里，这又快又猛但也让人担心。同时，科学家第一次让我们能‘听到’地球本身的各种低沉声音，比如冰山断裂，这能帮我们更好地了解地球。
aiBroadcastScript:
[这条是什么]
美国军方使用AI工具辅助锁定伊朗打击目标；科学家首次将地球次声波转为可听声音。

[翻成人话]
打仗用上了AI助手来快速找目标；我们第一次能‘听见’地球冰川、风暴发出的低沉‘心跳’声。

[为什么重要]
AI进入军事决策核心，可能改变战争方式但风险巨大；‘听’到地球次声波能革新环境与灾害监测。

[下一步确认点]
关注国际社会对军事AI的监管谈判；留意次声波监测技术如何应用于地震、火山预警等实际场景。
